posts.text:
🧭 Data Engineer Roadmap — What Actually Matters
When I first looked at a Data Engineering roadmap,
it felt overwhelming.
Too many tools. Too many concepts. No clear direction.
But over time, I realized:
👉 It’s not about learning everything.
👉 It’s about understanding the order and purpose.
Here’s how I now see the roadmap:
🔹 1. SQL & Data Fundamentals
Everything starts here.
If you don’t understand how data is stored, queried, and structured → nothing else will make sense.
🔹 2. Python / PySpark
Not just coding—but processing data at scale.
This is where you move from small datasets → big data thinking.
🔹 3. Data Modeling & ETL/ELT
This is where engineering actually begins.
Designing how data flows, transforms, and becomes usable.
🔹 4. Big Data Tools (Spark, Kafka)
Handling volume and velocity.
Learning how distributed systems process massive datasets.
🔹 5. Cloud Platforms (Azure/AWS/GCP)
Modern data engineering lives in the cloud.
Scalability, storage, and compute all come together here.
🔹 6. Orchestration (ADF, Airflow)
Pipelines don’t run themselves.
This layer controls scheduling, dependencies, and execution.
🔹 7. Data Lakes & Warehouses
Where data is stored and served.
From raw → structured → analytics-ready.
🔹 8. Monitoring & Data Quality
Pipelines will fail. Data will break.
Good engineers detect and fix issues early.
🔹 9. Real-Time Processing
For systems that need instant insights.
(Used when batch is not enough)
🔹 10. CI/CD & DevOps for Data
Taking pipelines from development → production reliably.
💡 What most people miss:
This is NOT a checklist.
👉 It’s a progression of thinking:
• From querying → processing
• From coding → designing systems
• From working code → reliable pipelines
💡 My biggest takeaway:
You don’t need to learn everything at once.
👉 Focus on one stage → build something → move forward
Because in the end:
👉 Data Engineering is not about tools.
It’s about building systems that handle data reliably at scale.

Found this helpful? Repost it! 🔁

#DataEngineering #Roadmap #Azure #BigData #CareerGrowth

posts.text:
LLM, RAG, AI Agent, Agentic AI.
If you can’t say when to use each, you are not ready for 2026.

I see teams mixing these up every week.

Confusion wastes time and money.

Here’s how I break down these systems (and what each one is ACTUALLY for):

→ LLM (Large Language Model)
Generates text from your prompt.
No outside info, fast and simple.
Limited to what it learned before.

→ RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)
Combines LLM power with real-time data retrieval.
Pulls in up-to-date info, but depends on quality sources.

→ AI Agent
Plans, reasons, and uses tools to complete tasks.
Works on multi-step workflows, remembers context, follows your goals.

→ Agentic AI
Multiple agents work together, divide tasks, share memory.
Handles complex, ongoing projects with feedback and adaptation.

Knowing the difference decides if your business stays ahead.

Here’s how I match them to real use-cases:

· LLM: Simple text generation, basic Q&A, chatbots.
· RAG: Research, summarizing fresh info, domain knowledge.
· AI Agent: Automating tasks that need planning and tool use.
· Agentic AI: Multi-user, multi-step processes, coordinated teams.

I learned the hard way:

Picking the wrong system wastes resources and slows your business.

Getting crystal clear on requirements before building helped me.

AI skills alone are not enough in 2026.

The winners will bridge business needs with the right AI architecture.

Want to get clear on what your team needs?

Hit me with a DM.

postedAt.postedAgoText: 1 month ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

posts.text:
RAG is not a “technique.”

It’s a whole ecosystem — and a developer’s stack.

Over the past 18 months, Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has quietly evolved from a simple “search + LLM” pattern into a complete engineering discipline.

Today, building AI systems isn’t just about choosing a model.

It’s about architecting the entire pipeline — from extraction to embeddings, from vector databases to evaluation, from closed models to open-source innovators.

repost.content:
I’m honored to be part of this innovative program focused on intelligent projects for road safety, in collaboration with the National Road Safety Observatory (ONSR), the Ministry of Interior, and the United Nations.

Our project is a VR serious game designed for traffic police training, integrating Reinforcement Learning and Virtual Reality to create immersive and intelligent learning experiences.

I would like to sincerely thank my supervisor, Mr Amine Kchiche , PMP®, for his guidance and continuous encouragement, as well as my teammate  Hamza Jemaa for his dedication, hard work, and commitment throughout this journey.

Together, we strive to contribute to a safer and better future for Tunisia 🇹🇳

#RoadSafety #Innovation #VirtualReality #ReinforcementLearning #SmartProjects #Tunisia #ONSR #TechForGood

posts.text:
Le dressing qui prend la poussière, c’est plus qu’un espace encombré : c’est une source de gaspillage textile.  

Chaque saison, des milliers de vêtements restent inutilisés, finissent à la poubelle ou sont incinérés, générant des émissions de CO₂ et une pression supplémentaire sur les ressources naturelles.  

Imaginez un espace virtuel où chaque pièce trouve rapidement une seconde vie. Une application web permet à chaque utilisateur de créer son « vide‑dressing », de mettre en ligne les vêtements qu’il ne porte plus et de les proposer à d’autres particuliers en Tunisie. Le processus est sécurisé : paiement en ligne, gestion des expéditions, tout est intégré.  

En facilitant la revente d’articles d’occasion, la plateforme réduit la demande de production neuve, diminue les déchets textiles et encourage une consommation circulaire. C’est un petit geste individuel qui, multiplié, crée un impact environnemental réel.  

Vous avez déjà un dressing qui déborde ? Pensez à le transformer en ressource pour d’autres et participez à la réduction de l’empreinte carbone du secteur de la mode.  

Quel vêtement pourriez‑vous libérer dès aujourd’hui pour donner une seconde vie à votre dressing ?  

#Sustainability #CircularEconomy #Ecommerce #Innovation #FashionTech

posts.text:
📌𝗨𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗿𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗔𝗜 𝗦𝘁𝗮𝗰𝗸: 𝗙𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗼 𝗔𝘂𝘁𝗼𝗻𝗼𝗺𝘆
In AI discussions, terms like Machine Learning, Deep Learning, Generative AI, RAG, and AI Agents are often presented separately.
In reality, they form a progressive, interconnected stack, where each layer builds upon and amplifies the previous one.
➡️ 𝐊𝐞𝐲 𝐋𝐚𝐲𝐞𝐫𝐬:
✔️ ML: Learn from data and detect patterns
✔️ DL: Handle complex tasks like vision, speech, NLP
✔️ Generative AI: Create new content (text, code, images)
✔️ RAG: Integrate external data for accurate, context-aware outputs
✔️ AI Agents: Plan, reason, and act autonomously
💡 𝗞𝗲𝘆 𝗜𝗻𝘀𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁:
 The progression of AI capabilities can be summarized as:
  𝐋𝐞𝐚𝐫𝐧𝐢𝐧𝐠 → 𝐂𝐨𝐦𝐩𝐥𝐞𝐱𝐢𝐭𝐲 → 𝐆𝐞𝐧𝐞𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 → 𝐆𝐫𝐨𝐮𝐧𝐝𝐢𝐧𝐠 → 𝐀𝐮𝐭𝐨𝐧𝐨𝐦𝐲
⚙️ 𝐅𝐫𝐨𝐦 𝐓𝐫𝐚𝐝𝐢𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧𝐚𝐥 𝐭𝐨 𝐌𝐨𝐝𝐞𝐫𝐧 𝐀𝐈 𝐖𝐨𝐫𝐤𝐟𝐥𝐨𝐰𝐬
Traditional: Input → Model → Output
Modern AI: Goal → Planning → Data Retrieval → Reasoning → Action
🎯 𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝗧𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗠𝗮𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗿𝘀:
Building robust AI today is not just about using models it’s about designing complete, reliable systems that integrate:
➡️ Learning behavior of models
➡️ Data and retrieval pipelines
➡️ External knowledge sources
➡️ Structured decision-making workflows
The true competitive edge is in system-level design, not just tool usage.
#AI #MachineLearning #DeepLearning #GenerativeAI #RAG #AIAgents #MLOps #DataScience
